original_abstract: In response to salinity stress, plants must manage the uptake and internal distribution of Na⁺ to balance osmotic requirements against the risk of ion toxicity. This review examines the complex orchestration of Na⁺ fluxes across various cellular membranes and tissues. While high levels of cytoplasmic Na⁺ can severely impair metabolic processes, salt-tolerant species maintain homeostasis not necessarily through novel transport proteins, but through the sophisticated regulation of existing flux pathways. We discuss the key mechanisms involved in Na⁺ entry via non-selective cation channels and HKT-type transporters, as well as its active extrusion and vacuolar sequestration mediated by Na⁺/H⁺ antiporters such as SOS1 and NHX1. Additionally, the paper addresses long-distance transport, including xylem loading, retrieval by HKT1;1-type proteins, and the physiological significance of phloem recirculation and specialized salt-secreting organs. We synthesis current knowledge on the signaling cascades—including calcium transients, cyclic nucleotides (cGMP), reactive oxygen species, and hormonal cues—that translate environmental salt sensing into transcriptional and post-translational adjustments. By summarizing these early-stage regulatory strategies, this work highlights the potential for engineering salt resilience in crops through the targeted manipulation of regulatory networks rather than single-gene traits.
humanized_abstract: Salt-exposed plants experience many physiological and developmental changes due to regulation of the sodium ion (Na⁺) in order to deal with osmotic demands as well as ion toxicity. There is a pressing need to understand the integration and regulation of Na⁺ transport within and between cells as well as between different plant organs. Understanding where Na⁺ entry occurs, particularly the molecular basis involved in non-selective cation channel permeability and the roles of the HKT transporters, is important as is the role of Na⁺ efflux and the resultant vacuolar storage. Current understanding of xylem loading, the function of the HKT1;1-type salt-retaking transporters, the role of phloem Na⁺ re-translocation as well as salt glands and leaves will also be discussed. Understanding at what level environmental salt signal is processed into corresponding physiological plant responses is also required. It is an area that has seen tremendous advancement in recent years including roles for calcium, cyclic nucleotides (cGMP), reactive oxygen species and a multitude of hormones associated with salt-stress acclimation and signaling. Summarising and bringing together some of the latest information associated with early salt-signalling in plant cells that contribute to gene expression that may influence salt- tolerance, this review discusses the potential of a systems approach for designing improved salt tolerance in agronomic crop species through the manipulation of regulatory pathways.
